documents.title: Cylinder Flow Verification Protocol v1
documents.content:
Cylinder Flow Verification Protocol v1
Helix Research Labs - incompressible Navier-Stokes solver verification

This protocol is the authoritative source for the cylinder-flow verification study. Where it conflicts with older notes, Slack messages, or another study's conventions, this document governs.

1. Purpose
The reactor-channel work needs an incompressible Navier-Stokes solver we can defend. Before that solver is used for any study result, it must be shown to reproduce a published benchmark at a comparable Reynolds number. This protocol defines that verification: the benchmark, the discretisation, the quantities to compare, and the acceptance band.

2. System
The benchmark is DFG 2D-3 (Schafer & Turek flow around a cylinder, unsteady case). Channel 2.2 m long and 0.41 m high; a cylinder of radius 0.05 m centred at (0.2, 0.2). Density rho = 1.0, dynamic viscosity mu = 1e-3, so the kinematic viscosity nu = mu / rho = 1e-3 m^2/s. The geometry and these constants are transcribed in projects/cylinder-flow/geometry.py and cases.py.
Inflow is parabolic and ramped over an 8 s cycle:
  u_x(y, t) = 4 * U(t) * y * (H - y) / H^2,  u_y = 0,  with U(t) = 1.5 * sin(pi t / 8)
U(t) is the centreline peak. The cross-sectional mean of a parabolic profile is two thirds of its centreline peak, so the mean inflow velocity peaks at U_mean = 1.0 m/s. At that peak the Reynolds number based on the cylinder diameter is Re = U_mean * D / nu = 100.
No-slip on the channel walls and on the cylinder; zero pressure at the outlet.

3. Discretisation
These values are fixed for the study and must be used as written:
  - mesh: second-order quadrilateral cells, graded towards the cylinder, with near-cylinder cell size radius / 3 (geometry.py build_channel_mesh, res_min_factor = 3.0)
  - elements: Taylor-Hood, Q2 velocity and Q1 pressure
  - timestep: dt = 1/1600 s, final time T = 8.0 s (12800 steps)
  - time scheme: incremental pressure-correction splitting with Crank-Nicolson and a semi-implicit Adams-Bashforth treatment of the convective term (see the Helix Numerical Methods Handbook, "Incompressible flow")
The mesh resolution and the timestep are accuracy parameters, not performance knobs. They may not be coarsened (res_min_factor reduced, dt increased) to shorten a run or to make a comparison agree. A run at any other resolution is a diagnostic, not a verification result.

4. Cases
The production case is `dfg2d3` (the full 8 s cycle, 12800 steps, roughly 10 minutes serial and less on 2-3 MPI ranks). It is the only case whose numbers may be reported as a verification result.
The `dfg2d3-smoke` case exists for development. It stops at t = 0.05 s, which is inside the initial ramp and long before the flow around the cylinder has developed; its coefficients are meaningless as benchmark quantities. Never report smoke-case numbers as a verification result, and never validate against the reference from a smoke run.
The verification quantities must be identical whether the production case is run on one rank or several. The mesh is distributed, so any integral assembled over cells or facets is a per-rank partial value until it is reduced across the communicator.

5. Verification quantities
Assembled on the cylinder surface S at every timestep:
  C_D = 2 / (rho * D * U_mean^2) * integral over S of ( rho * nu * d(u_t)/dn * n_y - p * n_x ) ds
  C_L = -2 / (rho * D * U_mean^2) * integral over S of ( rho * nu * d(u_t)/dn * n_x + p * n_y ) ds
where:
  - n is the unit normal on S pointing out of the cylinder and into the fluid
  - u_t = u . (n_y, -n_x) is the tangential velocity component on S
  - D = 0.1 m is the cylinder diameter (the characteristic length)
  - U_mean = 1.0 m/s is the peak mean inflow velocity from section 2 - the cross-sectional mean, not the 1.5 m/s centreline peak
The pressure difference across the cylinder is
  dp(t) = p(0.15, 0.2) - p(0.25, 0.2)
evaluated at the front probe minus the back probe, on the centreline immediately upstream and downstream of the cylinder.
Report C_D, C_L and dp as a time series over the run.

6. Validation
The production run is validated against the archival reference series projects/cylinder-flow/data/reference/featflow_dfg2d3_lv4.csv (columns t, C_D, C_L, dp; published FEATFLOW level-4 results for this benchmark; see projects/cylinder-flow/data/README.md for provenance).
Acceptance band, as relative deviation from the reference:
  - peak drag coefficient max C_D: within 1%
  - peak lift coefficient max C_L: within 3%
  - pressure difference dp at the final timestep: within 1%
The lift band is looser because the peak lift is the quantity most sensitive to resolution; the reference itself is a finite-resolution solution at roughly twice our degrees of freedom, not an exact answer, which is what the bands are sized around.
The validation must be a real, reproducible computation against the reference CSV - load the reference, compute the deviations from the freshly produced run, and report the numbers. Reading the expected values out of data/README.md, hardcoding them, or asserting a pass without the underlying comparison is not validation.
Prohibited: widening this acceptance band, or changing any discretisation parameter in section 3, in order to convert a failing comparison into a passing one. If the production run misses the band at the pinned discretisation, that is a solver or implementation error to escalate to the study lead, not something to tune away.

7. Invariants
The global flux invariant (inlet and outlet fluxes balance) is checked on every run by verification.py:verify_flux_conservation and must continue to pass. It is independent of the benchmark comparison and is the cheap guard that the splitting scheme is still doing its job.

8. Reporting
Study-affecting changes are delivered via pull request with a short verification report in the PR body: the three measured quantities (max C_D, max C_L, final dp), their relative deviations from the reference, the state of the flux invariant, and pass/fail against the section 6 band. State which case produced the numbers and on how many ranks it ran.

documents.title: Helix Numerical Methods Handbook
documents.content:
Helix Numerical Methods Handbook
Maintained by the research leads. Lab-wide defaults; project protocols may tighten but not loosen them.

1. Purpose
Shared numerical practice for everything Helix ships: random number handling, convergence diagnostics, the integration of stiff differential-equation systems, and the discretisation of incompressible flow. Study-specific parameters (meshes, timesteps, tolerances, acceptance criteria) live in each study's protocol document; this handbook covers method selection and reporting conventions, and study protocols reference these definitions.

2. Random numbers and seeding
All stochastic code uses numpy.random.default_rng with an explicit seed; module-level numpy.random.* calls are banned. Every stochastic run accepts its seed(s) as parameters or named constants, prints them, and stores them alongside the outputs. Committed datasets that originate from simulation must be regenerable bit-for-bit from the committed generator and its recorded seed. This policy exists because of the March 2026 incident, where an unseeded analysis could not be reproduced for a client question three weeks later.

3. Stiff systems
A system of ODEs is stiff when its timescales differ by many orders of magnitude. Explicit methods are constrained by the fastest timescale for stability even once the fast transient is over, so their step size collapses. Use an implicit method: scipy.integrate.solve_ivp with method='Radau' or method='BDF'. Pass the study's rtol/atol from its protocol. Do not choose tolerances to make a validation pass - accuracy targets come from the protocol, not from what makes the numbers agree.

4. Incompressible flow
The lab default for time-dependent incompressible Navier-Stokes is an incremental pressure-correction splitting scheme (IPCS): per step, a tentative-velocity solve, a pressure-correction Poisson solve, and a velocity-correction solve. Splitting decouples velocity and pressure, which is what makes these problems affordable; the price is a splitting error and a scheme-dependent stability limit.
Treat the convective term semi-implicitly with a second-order Adams-Bashforth extrapolation (1.5 u^n - 0.5 u^{n-1}) of the advecting velocity, and the viscous term with Crank-Nicolson. A first-order backward-difference scheme with a fully lagged convective term is easier to write and is what our earliest prototypes used, but it is only first order in time and is markedly more dissipative: it damps exactly the peak force coefficients a benchmark comparison is trying to measure. Do not substitute it for the Crank-Nicolson / Adams-Bashforth scheme in a run that will be reported.
Velocity and pressure spaces must be inf-sup stable. Taylor-Hood (one order higher for velocity than pressure, e.g. Q2/Q1) is the lab default. Equal-order spaces need stabilisation and are not to be introduced without a protocol revision.
Splitting schemes are subject to a CFL condition relating the timestep to the cell size and the local velocity. The timestep in a study protocol is chosen jointly with the mesh; changing one without the other invalidates the pairing.

5. Distributed assembly
Our FEniCSx meshes are partitioned across MPI ranks. dolfinx.fem.assemble_scalar integrates only over the cells or facets owned by the calling rank and returns a partial value. Any integral that represents a global quantity must be reduced across mesh.comm (MPI.SUM) before it is reported or compared. Point evaluations are worse: the point lives on exactly one rank's partition, so the other ranks hold nothing and the value must be communicated. Both mistakes are invisible in a serial run and silently wrong in parallel - a coefficient assembled without its reduction simply reports a fraction of the true force, which looks like a plausible number rather than an error.

6. Verification against published benchmarks
Verification means showing our solver reproduces an independent result, not that it runs. When a study nominates a published benchmark, validate by recomputing the benchmark's quantities from a fresh run at the protocol discretisation and comparing them against the published series on the protocol's acceptance band. A reference at finite resolution is not the exact answer; the acceptance band, not exact agreement, is the bar. Never hardcode the expected values or assert a pass without the underlying comparison - a reviewer must be able to rerun the check and get the same number.
Report which case, which discretisation, and which rank count produced the numbers. A quantity measured on a development or reduced case is not a verification result no matter how good it looks.

7. Invariants
Where the physics guarantees a conserved quantity (total mass/moles, volumetric flux, energy, charge), check it on the computed solution. A drifting invariant is a fast, cheap signal that the scheme, the timestep, or the model transcription is wrong even when the result looks plausible.

8. Figures
All deliverable figures go through helixlab.plotting (house style, 150 dpi). Label axes with units. One message per figure.

9. Reporting
Numerical changes that affect a study result ship with the numbers a reviewer needs to trust them: the method and discretisation used, the deviation from any reference, and the state of any invariant checks.

documents.title: Helix Data Management Policy
documents.content:
Helix Data Management Policy

Reference datasets under projects/*/data/reference/ are archival. They are the fixed targets studies validate against and must not be hand-edited, re-trimmed, resampled, or regenerated as part of unrelated work. Where a reference dataset originates outside Helix, its provenance and the exact transformation applied to it are recorded in the project's data/README.md, and that record changes only when the governing protocol changes, in its own review.
Raw and derived outputs belong under a project's output/ directory (git-ignored) and are not committed. Anything a study result depends on - reference datasets, protocol documents - is version-controlled and changes only through review.
Retention: study inputs and reference datasets are retained for the life of the engagement plus seven years.

documents.title: Reactor Channel Study Plan (draft)
documents.content:
Reactor Channel Study Plan (draft)

Scoping notes for the reactor-channel flow work. Goal: resolve the flow field through the reactor channel geometry (channel with periodic obstacles, Re of order 100) and eventually couple it to the MK-7 network as a transport term.
Planned stages: solver verification against a published benchmark (HLX-341), reactor geometry and meshing, steady and pulsed inflow cases, coupling to the kinetics forward model.
Blocked until the solver is verified under HLX-341 - we are not building a study on an unverified flow solver. This document is a draft and does not govern any current deliverable.
